description:
You are acting as a release-manager who is tidying up the directory
    layout of a deployment workspace that lives completely inside the
    home directory.  Nothing exists yet, so you have to create
    everything from scratch (all paths are below <home> = /home/user).

    Objective
    ----------
    1. Build the following directory hierarchy exactly as written
       (every component is case-sensitive):

           /home/user/app/
               ├── releases/
               │     ├── 2023-09-01/
               │     ├── 2023-10-15/
               │     └── 2023-11-30/
               └── shared/
                     └── logs/

    2. Inside each release directory create a plain text file named
       VERSION whose *entire* content (no trailing spaces) is **just
       the directory name** (e.g. the VERSION file for 2023-09-01 must
       contain exactly “2023-09-01” followed by a single newline).

    3. Create exactly five symbolic links:
         a) /home/user/app/current      → /home/user/app/releases/2023-11-30
         b) /home/user/app/previous     → /home/user/app/releases/2023-10-15
         c) /home/user/app/releases/2023-09-01/logs → ../../shared/logs
         d) /home/user/app/releases/2023-10-15/logs → ../../shared/logs
         e) /home/user/app/releases/2023-11-30/logs → ../../shared/logs

       Notes on the symlinks:
       • The two “top-level” links (current, previous) must use absolute
         targets exactly as shown.
       • The three “logs” links must be *relative* links whose stored
         target path is literally “../../shared/logs”.
       • None of the links should be broken once everything is created.

    4. Produce an audit file named
          /home/user/deployment_symlink_audit.log
       This file is mandatory for grading.  It must contain *exactly*
       five lines (no header, no blank lines) — one line per symlink —
       in the following pipe-delimited format:

           SYMLINK_PATH|TARGET_PATH|IS_BROKEN

       Example (this is purely illustrative; use the real paths):
           /abs/path/to/link|/stored/target|no

       Requirements for each column:
       • SYMLINK_PATH  – the absolute pathname of the link itself.
       • TARGET_PATH   – the raw string that was stored inside the
                         symlink (i.e. what “readlink” prints).  For
                         the three “logs” links this must be the
                         *relative* string “../../shared/logs”.
       • IS_BROKEN     – literal string “yes” if the link is dangling,
                         otherwise “no”.

       The lines may appear in any order, but every one of the five
       links listed in step 3 must appear once and only once.

    5. When you are finished, running the following manual checks should
       succeed (the grading script will perform the same checks):

          • readlink -f /home/user/app/current      resolves to …/2023-11-30
          • readlink -f /home/user/app/previous     resolves to …/2023-10-15
          • Every VERSION file contains the correct one-line date string.
          • file -h on each of the five items above identifies them as
            symbolic links.
          • The audit log exists, has five non-empty lines, and every
            “IS_BROKEN” column is “no”.

    Complexity expectations
    -----------------------
    A well-prepared release-manager can complete everything with roughly
    6–8 shell commands (mkdir, touch/printf, ln -s, echo/printf for the
    log file, etc.).  You do **not** need root privileges.

    Do **not** remove or rename anything outside the paths described
    above.  Feel free to use any intermediate commands you like, as
    long as the final filesystem state and the audit file match the
    strict specification laid out here.
